description:
You are a build-and-release engineer who has just finished compiling version 2.3.0 of the “appcli” command-line tool.  
    A colleague has already placed the unsigned binaries and the draft changelog in  

        /home/user/artifacts_staging  

    The staging directory currently contains exactly the following three plaintext files:  

        1. /home/user/artifacts_staging/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin  
        2. /home/user/artifacts_staging/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin  
        3. /home/user/artifacts_staging/CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md  

    Your task is to prepare the official release directory tree and generate a checksum manifest so that downstream automation can verify the integrity of every published file.

    Required end-state of the filesystem
    ------------------------------------
    1. A new directory “/home/user/releases/v2.3.0” must exist.  It is the **release root**.
    2. Inside the release root, create the two architecture-specific sub-directories shown below and relocate the binaries into them **without changing the original filenames**:  

           /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/linux_x86_64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin  
           /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/linux_arm64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin  

    3. Copy the file “CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md” from the staging area into the release root (do not rename it).  The final path must therefore be  

           /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md  

    4. Produce a file called  

           /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/manifest.txt  

       that lists the SHA-256 checksum of **every regular file that lives directly inside**
       “/home/user/releases/v2.3.0” **or any of its sub-directories**.  
       • Use the BSD/Coreutils `sha256sum` output style:  

             <64-hex-digit checksum><two spaces><relative path starting with ./><newline>  

       • Paths in the manifest **must be written relative to the release root and must start with “./”**.  
       • Sort the lines **alphabetically by the relative path** so that automated diff tools do not have to cope with nondeterministic ordering.  
       • The manifest must end with a single trailing newline and **nothing else** (no header, timestamp, comments, etc.).  

       Example layout (the actual checksums will be different):  

             d2c7...e3  ./CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md
             a6b1...5c  ./linux_arm64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin
             0f27...9d  ./linux_x86_64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin

    5. Leave nothing behind in “/home/user/artifacts_staging” except, possibly, empty directories.  
       The three source files must no longer live in that location once the task is complete.

    What the automated checker will validate
    ----------------------------------------
    • The exact directory layout described above exists.  
    • The three files are present at the exact paths given, and the staging area no longer contains them.  
    • “manifest.txt” exists, contains exactly three lines, each line matches the `<sha256><two-spaces><path>` format, the lines are alphabetically sorted, and **each checksum in the manifest matches the real SHA-256 digest of the corresponding file**.  
    • The file ends with a single trailing newline and no extra whitespace.

    Notes & Constraints
    -------------------
    • You do NOT have root privileges; stay under /home/user.  
    • All operations must be done using standard Linux command-line utilities available in a typical minimal environment (coreutils, find, etc.).  
    • Do not hard-code checksum values—compute them so that the manifest reflects the actual bytes on disk.  
    • The agent is free to use subshells, pipelines, or run background jobs, but the final on-disk state **must** match everything described above.  
    • Be mindful of relative vs. absolute paths when generating the manifest so that the checker’s path-comparison logic works.

    Deliverables
    ------------
    At the end of the session the system should contain:
      • /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/linux_x86_64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin  
      • /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/linux_arm64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin  
      • /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md  
      • /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/manifest.txt  

    That’s it—once the manifest has been generated and verified against the files, the release is ready to be pushed to your download server.
truth:
<!--
    Initial files shipped with the image (before the agent starts):

      /home/user/artifacts_staging/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin
          Content (exact, including trailing newline):
              "appcli binary for x86_64 version 2.3.0\n"

      /home/user/artifacts_staging/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin
          Content (exact, including trailing newline):
              "appcli binary for arm64 version 2.3.0\n"

      /home/user/artifacts_staging/CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md
          Content (exact, including trailing newline at the end):
              "# Changelog for v2.3.0\n\n- Feature A\n- Bug fix B\n"

    The automated test will:

      1. Verify that the three files above have been removed from
         /home/user/artifacts_staging (using `! -e` checks).

      2. Recompute checksums with: 
            cd /home/user/releases/v2.3.0 && sha256sum --binary \
                ./CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md \
                ./linux_arm64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin \
                ./linux_x86_64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin

      3. Read /home/user/releases/v2.3.0/manifest.txt.  It must:
            • Contain exactly three newline-separated lines,
              each matching the regex '^[0-9a-f]{64}  \./.+$'
            • Lines must be sorted (using `LC_ALL=C sort -c`).
            • For every listed file, the checksum in the manifest must
              equal the recomputed checksum from step 2.

      4. Verify that no extra files exist inside /home/user/releases/v2.3.0
         other than: manifest.txt, CHANGELOG-2.3.0.md,
         linux_x86_64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-x86_64.bin,
         linux_arm64/appcli-2.3.0-linux-arm64.bin,
         and the two architecture sub-directories themselves.

    By defining the checks this way, the test harness never needs the
    literal checksum strings in advance, keeping the public description
    free of secret data while still enabling deterministic validation.
    -->
